figures.caption: FIG. 1. Principal sketch of the workflow of the Quantum-enhanced MCMC method for the synthetic generation of Lagrangian tracer trajectories in a turbulent boundary layer above the ground and its comparison with a classical MCMC method. The velocity fluctuations u′(x, t) about a mean turbulent shear flow ⟨u⟩ are obtained from simulations. They are used to construct the target distribution of the acceleration p(a) for the sampling. The contour plot (upper left) shows the turbulent kinetic energy of the velocity field, taken from a snapshot of the lower half of a channel simulation [8]. Two initially very close tracer tracks are sketched. Their distance grows with time due to turbulent mixing. The red box below illustrates the stochastic particle advection method. The proposal distribution is either obtained classically or by the quantum algorithm, see blue box to the right. Note that the QE-MCMC algorithm is a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm.

FIG. 2. Quantum circuit of the distribution Q sketched with d×Nq qubits. The operator V̂ consists of p layers of rotation operators R̂X(2θ) followed by a unitary ÛC(γ) that entangles all qubits in each layer and follows from Ĥprob. The layers of R̂X are connected to Ĥmix.

FIG. 3. Comparison of dispersion curves computed using the solution of the stochastic differential equations (26)–(29) (SDE), the classical MCMC and QE-MCMC scheme. In all cases, the dispersion curves have been obtained for 3000 individual Lagrangian particle tracks, which were initialized with slightly different initial vertical positions. (a) Streamwise pair dispersion Dx(t), (b) wall-normal pair dispersion Dy(t), (c) spanwise pair dispersion Dz(t), and (d) total pair dispersion D(t).

FIG. 4. Comparison of the spectral gap δ between classical MCMC and QE-MCMC as a function of the number of qubits per dimension Nq for the HSF case. Here, the number of layers is p = 10 in the quantum algorithm.

FIG. 5. Contours of the streamwise velocity component u+ x (x +, y+, z+, t+ ≈ 250) at a time instant. The horizontal slice cuts are taken at three distances from the wall, (a) in the viscous sublayer at y+ ≈ 10, (b) in the logarithmic region at y+ ≈ 60, and (c) in the center region at y+ ≈ 500. The midplane of the channel is at y = h, which corresponds to y+ = 1000. Data are taken from the Johns Hopkins Turbulence Database [8]. In each panel, we indicate the correlation scale ℓcorr, which increases with distance from the wall.

FIG. 6. Wall-normal mean profiles of essential turbulent properties plotted in wall units for 64 selected horizontal planes, that will be used for the models, in semi-logarithmic plots. (a) Mean streamwise velocity ⟨u+ x ⟩xz(y+). (b) Components of the symmetric Reynolds stress tensor ⟨u′+ i u′+ j ⟩xz(y+). (c) Plane-averaged mean Kolmogorov scale η+(y+) = η(y)/ℓ+ and mean kinetic energy dissipation rate ε+(y+) = ε(y)ℓ+/u3 τ . Note that the vertical grid resolution of the DNS is given with 512 nonuniform grid points, much finer than the selected 64 points, which are shown here.

FIG. 7. Statistics of the velocity fluctuations in the turbulent channel flow at different heights. Panels (a,b) display data for y+ ≈ 10, (d,e) for y+ ≈ 60, and (g,h) for y+ ≈ 500. Panels (a,d,g) show the joint conditional PDFs p (u′)

FIG. 8. Temporal evolution of two Lagrangian particles within a tracer pair over 105 steps which were generated by the QE-MCMC algorithm. They were initialized at X+(0) = [0, 500, L+ z /2] T and X̃+(0) = [0, 500 + 16η+, L+ z /2] T, respectively. The initial fluctuating velocities and accelerations were sampled as (u′(0),a(0))T ∼ N (0, Σ̂zz(y + 0 )).

FIG. 9. Lagrangian particle pair dispersion curves. We compare a classical stochastic model (SDE) with a classical and quantum-enhanced Markov chain Monte Carlo method. We seed 3000 Lagrangian tracer particles in pairs at initial positions X(k)+(0) = [0, 500, L+ z /2] T, X̃(k)+(0) = [0, 500 + 16η+, L+ z /2] T and sampled initial velocity and acceleration (u′ (k)(0),a(k)(0))T ∼ N (0, Σ̂zz(y + 0 )).

FIG. 10. Time traces of the three components of the velocity fluctuation vector field, u′+ i . We compare the solution of the stochastic differential equation (SDE), with the classical and quantum-enhanced Markov chain Monte Carlo method, (MCMC) and (QE-MCMC). The left column shows the time traces for 1000 time units. The right column displays a zoom. The legend at the top holds for all panels.

FIG. 11. Total particle pair dispersion curves for different initial seeding heights, which are indicated in the legend on top of the panels. (a) Classical MCMC algorithm. (b) Stochastic model. (c) Quantum-enhanced MCMC algorithm. The temperature parameter T was set to 3.4 for the two-dimensional and 3.9 for the one-dimensional distribution.

FIG. 12. Comparison of the effective spectral gap δ, as defined in Eq. (67), between classical MCMC and QE-MCMC as a function of the number of qubits per dimension Nq for the TCF case. Here, the number of layers is p = 10 in the quantum algorithm.

FIG. 13. Dependence of the effective spectral gap width on the sampling temperature T for a given qubit number 2 ≤ Nq ≤ 5. Top: Data for the joint PDF of a′ x and a′ y. Bottom: Data for the PDF of a′ z. In both panels, the spectral gap for the classical MCMC algorithm is added for comparison.
TABLE I. Wallclock time comparison of the three algorithms for the two cases, HSF and TCF. NPa is the number of particles and T is the number of time steps. All comparisons are run on a single CPU.
